coredata.dc:title: Electric-field-induced assists in the preferential extraction of lithium from spent LiFePO4
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of Lishui Institute of Ecology and Environment, Nanjing University.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of Lishui Institute of Ecology and Environment, Nanjing University.
coredata.dc:creator: Yong, Shangpei
Lin, Yan
coredata.dc:description: Due to the advantages of high energy density, high conversion efficiency, and rapid response speed associated with power lithium batteries, there has been a significant expansion in their use. However, industry forecasts indicate that if large quantities of waste lithium-ion batteries are not managed properly, it could lead to serious environmental pollution issues as these batteries reach the end of their life cycle. Therefore, developing highly efficient and environmentally friendly recycling technologies is urgently needed. This paper proposes a green and efficient selective lithium extraction process integrating leaching technology with electrochemical methods. This approach enables the separation of Li and FePO4 within an electrolyte through electric field assistance while effectively minimizing waste liquid generation. The effects of various factors on Li and Fe’s leaching rates were investigated using orthogonal and single factor experiments. Under optimal conditions, the leaching rate of Li reached 98.85%, while that for Fe was only 0.22%, resulting in a selectivity ratio (Li/Fe) of 99.78%, which demonstrates successful selective extraction of lithium. Furthermore, we assessed the feasibility of this experiment through potentiometric-pH diagrams. We elucidated its mechanism using XPS, XRD, and SEM. Unlike conventional destructive leaching methods, the Li extraction process facilitated by the electric-field-induced assistance resembles the charging process of LiFePO4 batteries. Ultimately, Li was recovered as Li2CO3, while Fe and P present in the slag were subsequently retrieved as FePO4 with battery-grade purity through a hydrothermal method. This study presents an environmentally sustainable and economically viable approach for recovering spent LiFePO4 batteries.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Spent Lithium iron phosphate battery
Recovering
Electric-field-induced
Preferential extraction
Lithium

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electric field-assisted leaching for lithium extraction
Recovery of lithium from leach solution
Regeneration of FePO4 from residue after lithium extraction
Characterization and mechanism of electric-field-induced assists

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Flow chart of electric field-assisted experiments.
(a) Raw material XRD pattern, (b) SEM and EDS images of raw materials.
Design and Analysis of Experiments by Orthogonal Arrays.

(a) Effect of liquid–solid ratio on the leaching rate of Li and Fe, (b) Effect of current density on the leaching rate of Li and Fe, (c) Effect of
H
2
SO4 concentration on the leaching rate of Li and Fe, (d) Effect of temperature on the leaching rate of Li and Fe.

(a) XRD pattern of lithium carbonate, (b) XRD pattern of leached residue, (c) XRD pattern of uncalcined precipitate, (d) XRD pattern of calcined precipitate, (e) Specific surface area of regenerated FePO4, (f) Particle size distribution of regenerated FePO4.
(a–b) SEM of raw materials, (c–d) SEM of raw materials, (e–h) EDS spectra of raw materials, (i–l) EDS spectra of leaching residues.

(a) Finishing XRD mapping of raw materials, (b) Finishing XRD mapping of leach residue, (c) pH-potential plot of Li-Fe-P-
H
2
O at 25 °C, (d) XPS profiles of feedstock and leaching residue, (e) Effect of normal leaching, potentiation assistance, and a combination of both on the leaching rate of Li and Fe.
(a–c) Leaching kinetics curves of spent LiFePO4 material by slurry electrolysis (d) Linear fitting of lnk vs. 1000/T for leaching of Li.
Table of orthogonal experiments and range analysis.

row.entry: H2SO4 concentration > L/S > Current density > Rotate speed > Electrode distance
H2SO4 concentration 0.2 mol/L, L/S 15 mL/g, Current density 20 mA/cm 2, Rotate speed 500 r/min, Electrode distance 7 cm

head.ce:title: Electric-field-induced assists in the preferential extraction of lithium from spent LiFePO4

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Metallurgy and Energy Engineering, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming 650093, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Metallurgy and Energy Engineering, Kunming University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Metallurgy and Energy Engineering, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming 650093, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Complex Nonferrous Metal Resources Clean Utilization, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming 650093, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Complex Nonferrous Metal Resources Clean Utilization, Kunming University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Complex Nonferrous Metal Resources Clean Utilization, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming 650093, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Faculty of Metallurgy and Energy Engineering, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming 650093, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Metallurgy and Energy Engineering, Kunming University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Due to the advantages of high energy density, high conversion efficiency, and rapid response speed associated with power lithium batteries, there has been a significant expansion in their use. However, industry forecasts indicate that if large quantities of waste lithium-ion batteries are not managed properly, it could lead to serious environmental pollution issues as these batteries reach the end of their life cycle. Therefore, developing highly efficient and environmentally friendly recycling technologies is urgently needed. This paper proposes a green and efficient selective lithium extraction process integrating leaching technology with electrochemical methods. This approach enables the separation of Li and FePO4 within an electrolyte through electric field assistance while effectively minimizing waste liquid generation. The effects of various factors on Li and Fe’s leaching rates were investigated using orthogonal and single factor experiments. Under optimal conditions, the leaching rate of Li reached 98.85%, while that for Fe was only 0.22%, resulting in a selectivity ratio (Li/Fe) of 99.78%, which demonstrates successful selective extraction of lithium. Furthermore, we assessed the feasibility of this experiment through potentiometric-pH diagrams. We elucidated its mechanism using XPS, XRD, and SEM. Unlike conventional destructive leaching methods, the Li extraction process facilitated by the electric-field-induced assistance resembles the charging process of LiFePO4 batteries. Ultimately, Li was recovered as Li2CO3, while Fe and P present in the slag were subsequently retrieved as FePO4 with battery-grade purity through a hydrothermal method. This study presents an environmentally sustainable and economically viable approach for recovering spent LiFePO4 batteries.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid development of new energy technologies, the utilization of lithium resources in batteries applications has become increasingly significant (Zhao et al., 2024). The performance advantages of lithium batteries render them an ideal power source for electric vehicles, which offer high energy efficiency, low operating costs, and favorable environmental impacts compared to traditional fuel-powered vehicles (Li et al., 2022; Liu and Wang, 2021). In the first half of 2023, the Ministry of Industry and Information Technology reported that continued grown in China’s lithium-ion battery industry. According to the announcements from industry standards organizations and enterprise information sources, lithium battery production in China exceeded 400 GWh during this period a year-on-year increase exceeding 43% with total revenue for the lithium battery sector, reaching approximately 600 billion RMB. By 2025, global scrap from lithium-ion batteries is expected to reach around 640,000 tonnes, with China’s share expected to be about 350,000 tonnes. Currently, lithium iron phosphate batteries constitute a substantial portion of power batteries; consequently, a significant number of used lithium iron phosphate batteries will require processing as they reach their end-of-life stage (Luo et al., 2024; Yang et al., 2021b). The content of Li in spent Li-ion batteries far exceeds that found in ore or even screened concentrates. Thus, recycling spent Li-ion batteries can mitigate the shortage of lithium resources while substantially reducing environmental pollution and damage. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop effective technologies for processing spent lithium iron phosphate batteries with maximized both value recovery and resource conservation.

Treating waste LFP materials can involve various methods, including hydrometallurgical leaching (Chen et al., 2019; Huang et al., 2022), pyro calcination (Qiu et al., 2024; Song et al., 2024), regeneration and repair (Zhang et al., 2022a; Zhao et al., 2024). Yang et al. (2021a) utilized citric acid as a reagent for lithium extraction from waste LFP batteries, achieving a remarkable leaching rate of 97.58% with lithium recovered in the form of lithium citrate. Li et al. (2017) employed an
H
2
SO4-
H
2
O2 leaching system, resulting in approximately 95.56% of lithium precipitating and recovering as Li3PO4. Additionally, high-temperature removal of carbon allowed for the direct recovery of FePO4 from the leaching slag. While current wet leaching methods demonstrate high recovery rates, they necessitate substantial volumed of wastewater during experimentation. Chen et al. (2016) implemented a high-temperature solid-phase repair technique to modify the recovered cathode powder by heat treatment at various temperatures for potential reuse. Their findings indicated that at a temperature of 650 °C, the current density exhibited discharge capacity and specific energy density comparable to that of unused cathode material, effectively demonstrating successful material repair suitable for reapplication in lithium-ion batteries. Shin et al. (2015) proposed an environmentally friendly recycling process specifically designed for LFP battery cathode materials utilizing a solution precipitation route. This method involved obtaining crystalline FePO4
⋅ 2H2O which was then combined with ground lithium and carbon sources before undergoing heat treatment at 700 °C for eight hours within a tube furnace to produce LiFePO4/C electrode materials. The primary cause behind the failure of cathode materials is attributed to structural collapse caused by the loss of Li
+
during the cycling process, which is the direct regeneration method recommendation for Li
+
replenishment and structural repair.

The process of regenerating used LFP cathode materials is highly demanding in terms of impurity control, and direct regeneration can only be achieved under stringent conditions regarding impurity content. Despite this challenge, the battery has undergone thousands of charge–discharge cycles, leading to structural degradation. Consequently, when employing regenerative repair methods, the performance of the repaired battery significantly lags behind that of the original material (Jia et al., 2023; Song et al., 2017; Zhou et al., 2022). Zhang et al. (2022b) investigated sulfate-free roasting for LFP recovery and found that NaHSO4
⋅
H
2
O facilitated lithium extraction from the Fe-P-O structure through thermodynamic calculations and thermal performance characterization. In this process, lithium was present as a water-soluble thiolate, while residual iron, phosphorus, and oxygen were incorporated into insoluble compounds. The capacity of the regenerated LFP cathode material derived from this recovered product reached 146 m Ah/g at a rate of 1 C. Dang et al. (2020) employed chlorination roasting followed by water leaching, then X-ray diffraction (XRD) results indicated that most LiAl(SiO3)2 in the slag had been converted to LiCl with a lithium recovery rate reaching up to 90.58%. However, it is imperative to note that fire recovery constitutes an energy-intensive procedure that generates noxious gases and dust. This reality necessitates the incorporation of purification devices an sulfuric acid that escalates costs while simultaneously diminishing recovery rates. The application of an electric field facilitates the migration and enrichment of metals through the movement of electrons within that field, offering advantages such as low operational costs and high processing efficiency (Li et al., 2020; Qin et al., 2024; Yang et al., 2023).

In this study, Li and FePO4 were selectively extracted from waste LiFePO4 utilizing current-assisted leaching techniques based on the potential-pH diagram of Li-Fe-P-
H
2
O. Electric-field-induced assists leaching enhances the leaching reaction by applying electric potential, changes the dissolution behavior of metal ions in the leaching process through the action of the electric field, promotes the reduction or oxidation reaction of metal ions by using applied voltage, changes the chemical state of the substance, and improves the leaching efficiency of the target metal. While the traditional electrochemical methods mainly achieve the separation of substances through current-driven chemical reactions in the electrolysis tank. This paper provides a comprehensive investigation into how various process parameters-including
H
2
SO4 concentration, current intensity, liquid–solid ratio, pole spacing, and rotational affect the leaching rates of lithium and iron. In solution, Li exists as an ion while Fe and P are present in solid form as FePO4, which can be efficiently separated via filtration. Following decontamination and evaporation processes to concentrate the higher lithium-containing solution obtained, lithium carbonate (Li2CO3) was precipitated by adding sodium carbonate (Na2CO3). The recovered Li2CO3, along with FePO4/C, was subsequently utilized to re-synthesize lithium iron phosphate.

After discharge and fine dismantling, this experiment utilized LFP cathode powder sourced from a battery recycling company. The raw materials used included
H
2
SO4 (purity 95%–98%),
H
2
O2 (purity ≥ 30%), and Na2SO4 (purity ≥ 99%), all procured from Chongqing Chuan Dong Chemical (Group) Co. All reagents were employed directly without further purification, with all solutions prepared using ultrapure water.

The experimental flow chart is illustrated in Fig. 1. The process begins with weighing the appropriate amount of waste LiFePO4 cathode powder, which is then immersed in a mixed solution consisting of
H
2
SO4,
H
2
O2, and Na2SO4. This mixed solution is adjusted to form a stable slurry using a thermostatically controlled magnetic stirrer. Subsequently, the slurry was introduced into graphite electrodes (5 cm*5 cm) and a constant current was applied to react for 3 h. Upon completion of the reaction, the suspension is immediately filtered and washed three times to separate the electrolyte from the leaching residue. The leaching residue is dried at 100 °C for 2 h. Following this step, the electrolyte undergoes evaporation and condensation until it reaches an appropriate concentration. The concentrated electrolyte is then added dropwise to a saturated Na2CO
3
solution through reverse titration, forming Li2CO3 upon filtration at the end of this reaction. Lithium residue secondary leaching to obtain Fe-containing solution, using hydrothermal precipitation of iron precipitation in the solution of Fe precipitation as FePO4.

At the conclusion of the experiment, the leaching rates of lithium and iron from the electrolyte were calculated as follows:
(1)
E =
C
M e
× V
m ×
A
M e
× 100 %
Where E represents the leaching rate of lithium and iron,
C
Me
(g/L) denotes the concentration of metal elements in the electrolyte, V (L) indicates the volume of the electrolyte, and m (g) refers to the mass of experimental raw materials added. Ame is the percentage of metals present in the raw material.

The selectivity of Li/Fe is calculated using the following equation:
(2)
D
L i / F e
=
(
C
L i
× V )
/ 6 . 94
(
C
L i
× V )
/ 6 . 94 +
(
C
F e
× V )
/ 55 . 85
D
Li/Fe
represents the extraction selectivity, which is calculated using relative molar mass. V denotes the volume of the electrolyte,
C
Li
indicates the Li concentration in the electrolyte, and
C
Fe
refers to the Fe concentration in the electrolyte. The value 6.94 corresponds to the relative atomic mass of lithium, while 55.85 represents the relative atomic mass of iron.

The phases of the solid samples were analyzed using (XRD RigakuXRDXMiniflex 600, Japan) over a range of 10-80° at a scanning rate of 1°/min. The morphological characteristics of the raw materials and leaching residues were examined through scanning electron microscopy (SEM JSM-IT210, JEOL). The composition of the leachate was characterized by inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectroscopy (ICP-OES Thermo Fisher Scientific, China). Additionally, the valence states of iron in both the raw material and leach residue were investigated using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS Esca Lab 250Xi).

XRF was used for semi-quantitative analysis of raw materials, and ICP-OES was used for quantitative analysis of Li and Fe. The elemental composition of the spent LFP cathode material is as follows: Li (4.08%), Fe (32.4%), P (24.4%), C (5.38%), and Others (33.74%). The XRD pattern, SEM image, and EDS image of the raw material are presented in Fig. 2(a) and Fig. 2(b), respectively. The primary component of the raw material is LiFePO4, which constitutes approximately 93.27% of the total mass, with its XRD pattern aligning closely with that of the standard reference card. Notably, no significant heterogeneous peaks were observed; additionally, the EDS image indicates a uniform distribution of phosphorus (P), iron (Fe), and oxygen (O) elements throughout the sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electric field-assisted leaching for lithium extraction

ce:section.ce:para:
The effects of
H
2
SO4 concentration, current density, liquid–solid ratio, pole spacing, and rotational speed on Li and Fe leaching rates were investigated through orthogonal experiments in the preferential lithium extraction process. The experimental conditions are summarized in Table 1,
H
2
SO4 concentration ranged from 0.2 to 0.6 mol/L with a gradient of 0.1 mol/L; current density varied between 16 and 32 mA/cm2 with a gradient of 4 mA/cm2; the liquid–solid ratio was set from 10 to 30 with a gradient of 5; pole spacing was adjusted from 3 to 7 cm with a gradient of 1 cm; and rotational speed ranged from 400 to 800 r/min with a gradient of 100 r/min. Ultimately, optimal conditions for preferential lithium extraction were determined based on significance analysis combined with one-way experiments. In the range analysis, the Li and Fe leaching rate data were processed by the combined scoring method with the following formula:
(3)
X =
E
L i
+
( 1 −
E
F e
)
Orthogonal experiments were initially conducted, and the resulting orthogonal experimental effect plot is presented in Fig. 3. The optimal combination of potentiometric aids was determined through range analysis, utilizing the polar deviation R to assess the influence of five test factors on the leaching rates of lithium and iron at various levels. The magnitude of the polar deviation R indicates how changes in factor levels affect test results; a larger value of R signifies that variations in a particular factor exert a more substantial impact on outcomes, while a smaller value suggests relatively minor effects. As shown in Table 1,
H
2
SO4 concentration, liquid–solid ratio, and current density significantly influence the leaching rates of lithium and iron, followed by rotational speed and pole spacing. In the electric-field-induced, the voltage difference between the electrodes (i.e., the electric field strength) determines the rate of migration of ions and the progress of the reaction, according to the electric field strength and the formula for the calculation of the resistance and pole spacing in an electrolytic cell:
(4)
E =
U
d
=
I R
d
(5)
R = ρ
d
A
(6)
E =
I ∗ ρ ∗
d
A
d
=
I ∗ ρ
A
Where E is the electric field strength, I is the current, R is the resistance, d is the pole spacing, ρ is the solution resistivity, and A is the effective contact area of the electrode.

The main influences on the field strength in the electrolytic cell are the current, the solution’s resistivity, and the effective contact area of the electrodes. The spacing of the electrodes does not have much influence on the field strength.

To refine these findings further, a one-factor experiment was performed based on initial orthogonal experiments. The gradients for
H
2
SO4 concentration, current density, and liquid–solid ratio were subdivided while temperature variables were incorporated to achieve more accurate and efficient experimental conditions.

The influence of the liquid–solid ratio on the leaching rates of Li and Fe was investigated by controlling the mass of lithium iron phosphate (LFP), thereby fixing the volume of liquid to regulate the liquid–solid ratio. The results are presented in Fig. 5a. Experimental findings indicate that the leaching rate of Fe in solution remains below 0.2%. Notably, both Li and Fe leaching rates exhibit a significant increase within the range of 10 mL/g to 17.5 mL/g. A higher liquid–solid ratio facilitates a reduction in Li
+
concentration within the bulk solution, enhancing its migration from the reaction interface to the bulk solution, which subsequently increases the leaching rate of Li
+
. As the liquid–solid ratio increases, there is a gradual decrease in LFP mass. Consequently, an excess presence of
H
2
SO4 and
H
2
O2 in solution can lead to a decrease in pH levels, resulting in concurrent leaching processes for Fe2＋ alongside those for Li
+
. However, when maintaining a liquid–solid ratio between 17.5 mL/g and 25 mL/g, a slight decline in Li
+
leaching rates is observed; this phenomenon can be attributed to excessive use of
H
2
O2 leading to partial oxidation of Fe2＋ to Fe3＋, which precipitates as Fe(OH)3. The formation of Fe(OH)3 not only adsorbs Li
+
present in solution-thereby diminishing its leaching rate-but also reduces overall Fe content within that same solution.

The influence of current density on the leaching rates of Li and Fe is illustrated in Fig. 4(b). The leaching rate of Li was consistently maintained above 95% at current density strengths ranging from 20 mA/cm2 to 28 mA/cm2. Specifically, within the range of 20 mA/cm2 to 22 mA/cm2, the leaching rate of Li ions increased, peaking at 97.30%. This enhancement can be attributed to the increase in current density with rising current levels, which accelerated ion migration within the electrolyte and facilitated anodic oxidation (Cao et al., 2021), thereby promoting a more efficient release of Li from LiFePO4. However, beyond a current intensity of 22 mA/cm2, there was a slight decrease in the leaching rate of Li. The elevation in current density also stimulated hydrogen precipitation reactions at the cathode and oxygen precipitation reactions at the anode. The resultant gas bubbles tended to adhere to the electrode surface, consequently reducing the effective contact area between lithium iron phosphate (LFP) and the electrode; this reduction contributed to a minor decline in both Li and Fe leaching rates (Vogel et al., 2020).

As illustrated in Fig. 4(c), this experiment investigated the impact of
H
2
SO4 concentration on lithium and iron leaching rates. The leaching rate of Li exhibited a significant increase from 55.63% to 96.91% as the
H
2
SO4 concentration was raised from 0.1 mol/L to 0.2 mol/L. Beyond an
H
2
SO4 concentration level of 0.2 mol/L, the lithium leaching rate stabilized at equilibrium, while the Fe leaching rate experienced a sharp increase. The enhanced conductivity provided by hydrogen ions, which are recognized as the most efficient electrical conductors among all ions, contributes to an increased number of electrons participating in reactions within the solution as the concentration rises; this phenomenon subsequently elevates the leaching rate of Fe. Furthermore, after reaching a concentration of 0.2 mol/L, the Li leaching rate approaches complete extraction, while FePO4 formed during the reaction interacts with excess H
+
, leading to gradual solubilization of Fe present in the precipitate form. To achieve high selectivity for lithium extraction and maximize separation between Li and Fe, we selected an
H
2
SO4 concentration level of 0.2 mol/L for our experiments.

Temperature plays a crucial role in influencing both ion migration and chemical reaction rates. As illustrated in Fig. 4(d), temperature significantly affects the leaching rates of Li and Fe. The leaching rate of Fe remains below 1% as temperature increases, while the leaching rate of Li rises between 20 °C and 50 °C, stabilizing after that at temperatures above 50 °C. According to Arrhenius’ law, an increase in temperature typically enhances the reaction rate. Elevated temperatures augment the kinetic energy of molecules, leading to increased collision frequency and sufficient collision energy among them, thereby facilitating chemical reactions. In terms of leaching processes, higher temperatures promote mineral dissolution and enhance leaching efficiency, resulting in increased leaching rates (Li et al., 2021). Furthermore, there is an inverse relationship between temperature and solution viscosity; elevated temperatures decrease solution viscosity, reduce resistivity within the solution, and accelerate ion and electron migration rates, ultimately contributing to a higher leaching rate for Li.

Under optimal conditions, the average leaching rate of Li was found to be 98.85%, while the average leaching rate of Fe was only 0.22% across three sets of repeated experiments. After the assisted leaching process, we filtered the mixture to obtain a black leaching residue and a clear yellowish liquid. Fig. 5 presents a comprehensive flow chart illustrating the potentiometric-assisted preferential extraction of lithium.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recovery of lithium from leach solution
ce:section.ce:para:
The recovery of Li was positively correlated with the concentration of Li
+
ions (Chen et al., 2018; Shin et al., 2022; Zhao et al., 2019). Consequently, the electrolyte was concentrated through evaporation. Following this process, the concentration of Li
+
reached 16.5 g/L. NaOH was subsequently added to adjust the pH to 11 in order to remove Fe, Al, and other impurities present in the solution. The concentrated electrolyte was then gradually introduced into a mixture containing anhydrous sodium carbonate (Na2CO3) at a dosage of 1.1 times the theoretical requirement via a peristaltic pump while stirring at a temperature of 95 °C for 30 mins. It is noteworthy that the solubility of lithium carbonate (Li2CO3) in water is inversely proportional to temperature; specifically, its solubility at 0 °C is measured at 1.54 g, whereas it decreases to 0.72 g at 100 °C. Therefore, hot water was used to clean after filtration to minimize the potential loss of Li2CO3. A white precipitate was obtained from this process and subsequently placed in an oven set at 100 °C for 2 h. XRD patterns of the final product, such as Fig. 6(a), show that the recovered product is pure and highly crystalline and matches the standard card of Li2CO3. The purity of Li2CO3 in this process was 98.52%, and its yield was about 75.73%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Regeneration of FePO4 from residue after lithium extraction
ce:section.ce:para:
The residue obtained following lithium extraction was subjected to further leaching under the following conditions: a
H
2
SO4 concentration of 2 mol/L, a reaction temperature of 60 °C, a reaction duration of 2 h, a hydrogen peroxide concentration of 30%, and a liquid-to-solid ratio of 7:1. During electric-field-induced assists leaching, a small amount of Al is present in the Li solution and most of the Al is present in the leach residue as NaAl(SO4)2
⋅ 12H2O. The most common water purification agent, which can form charged particles in the aqueous solution, adsorbs suspended matter (FePO4) in the water to form larger flocs accompanied by the separation of the iron phosphate slag from the solution. This process yielded a leaching solution rich in iron ions and phosphate ions. The concentration of Fe in the leaching solution was measured at 38.12 g/L, while the concentration of P was recorded at 20.85 g/L. The initial ratio of Fe to P in the leaching solution was determined to be 1.01. XRD analysis indicated that the unreacted residue primarily consisted of carbon (C), while Fe and P were almost entirely extracted during the leaching process, as demonstrated in Fig. 6(b). With a pH value of 1.5, a reaction temperature of 95 °C, a stirring speed of 300 r/min, and a reaction time of 2 h, the hydrothermal iron sinking conditions were established with an oxygen pressure (
P
O2
) of 0.8 MPa and NH3
⋅ H2O as the precipitant. Hydrothermal pressurization of iron precipitation is used. The main purpose of pressurization is to improve the reaction conditions and enhance the control of the reaction rate, product purity, and crystal structure by increasing the temperature and pressure, as well as to improve the solubility, the reactant activity, the growth rate of the crystals, and the homogeneity of the particles. Pressurization can complete the formation of crystal nuclei in a shorter period, while a shorter period will limit the growth of the product microcrystalline grains, the position being ultrafine powder to provide favorable conditions. Under these conditions, the precipitation rate of Fe reached 98.66%, resulting in a Fe/P ratio for the sample of 1.05, while the content of impurities such as copper and zinc remained below 0.05%. The XRD profile in Fig. 6(c) indicates that the precipitation is Fe2(NH4)(OH)(PO4)2 (
H
2
O)2. The precipitate underwent calcination, as indicated by the subsequent XRD analysis in Fig. 6(d). Compared with the standard card (PDF#77-0094), the calcined sample was identified as FePO4, which exhibited a strong diffraction peak and narrow width, indicating a well-defined crystal structure and high crystallinity. The specific surface area of the FePO4 sample, prepared under optimal pressurized hydrothermal conditions, was measured to be 5.7521 m2/g, and the detection results are presented in Fig. 6(e). A laser particle size analyzer was employed to assess recycled iron phosphate. The results indicated that the particle size distribution of FePO4 ranged from 1.24 to
5 . 86
μ m
, with an average particle size of
1 . 87
μ m
. In addition, the
D
10
value was measured at
1 . 38
μ m
, the
D
50
at
1 . 87
μ m
, the
D
90
at
2 . 59
μ m
, and the
D
99
at
3 . 69
μ m
, as illustrated in Fig. 6 (f). The vibration density was recorded at 0.709 g/mL, which meets the industry standard of battery and iron phosphate HG/T4701-2014.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and mechanism of electric-field-induced assists

ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 7 includes scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and energy-dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) spectra for raw materials and leaching residues. A comparison between Fig. 7(b) and Fig. 7(d) indicates no significant alterations in microscopic morphology between raw material and electrolytic slag; thus, their structures remain intact. Fig. 7(e)–(h) display EDS images of the feedstock while Fig. 7(i)–(l) present EDS images of the leaching residue; there are no notable differences observed in elemental distributions for Fe, P, and O. Unlike destructive leaching methods, electric field-assisted leaching for lithium extraction resembles that occurring during LiFePO4 charging processes (Madej and Wojciechowski, 2021). Throughout both the charging and discharging phases, LiFePO4 maintains structural stability; therefore, no apparent changes occur within its particles.

Fig. 8(a) shows the refined XRD mapping of the raw material. Fig. 8(b) presents the XRD spectrum of the leaching residue obtained under optimal conditions for electric field-assisted leaching, revealing that it consists of phosphorus ferromanganese ore FePO4 with an orthorhombic crystal structure (Burba and Frech, 2004). During the electric field assisted leaching process, a transformation in its physical phase occurs as Li from LiFePO4 is released into the solution; consequently, the LiFePO4 phase diminishes while a new iso-phosphorite phase of FePO4 emerges. Isophosphorus ferromanganese ore (Mn,Fe)PO4 is a metastable compound primarily formed by lithium removal during the charging of LiFePO4, exhibiting structural similarities to LiFePO4. As illustrated in the figure, prominent diffraction peaks are observed at approximately 18.1°, 20.6°, 23.7°, 25.8°, 30.8°, and 36.7° in the electrolytic slag’s XRD pattern, corresponding to those listed on the FePO4 standard card (PDF#34-0134). The average grain size was calculated to be approximately 50.0 nm using Jade software (19).

Fig. 8(c) illustrates the pH-potential diagram of Li-Fe-P-
H
2
O at a temperature of 25 °C. In pathway (2), LiFePO4 can decompose into Li
+
and Fe2＋ under low potential and pH conditions. Subsequently, Li
+
and FePO4 can be separated by increasing either the pH or redox potential through the addition of an alkali or oxidant. Similarly, in the pathway (3), LiFePO4 may decompose into Li
+
and Fe3＋ within solid acid environments that are strongly oxidizing; thereafter, separation of Li
+
from FePO4 can be achieved by directly raising the pH (by adding alkali) in the subsequent step. However, processes (2) and (3) require significant amounts of acid and alkali, leading to reagent wastage and environmental pollution due to waste liquid generation. In contrast, route (1) allows for the direct decomposition of LiFePO4 into Li
+
and FePO4 by elevating the redox potential either through oxidant addition or the application of an external electric field in an acidic environment. This approach mitigates reagent wastage and reduces environmental contamination while facilitating selective leaching of lithium ions. Concurrently, FePO4 remains in solid form within the slag.

Fig. 8(d) presents the XPS patterns of Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2 for both the raw material and the leaching residue. Notably, the peaks corresponding to Fe 2p3/2 are more pronounced and exhibit a larger area compared to those of Fe 2p1/2. The binding energies observed at 724.22 eV and 710.99 eV are characteristic of Fe2＋ in the raw material, while those at 726.24 eV and 712.36 eV correspond to Fe3＋ in the leaching residue (Chen et al., 2022). Furthermore, there is a shift in both the Fe 2p1/2 and Fe 2p3/2 peaks, indicating that nearly all of the Fe2＋ present in LiFePO4 was oxidized to Fe3＋ during the experiment. This observation suggests that throughout the experimental process, virtually all of the initial Fe2＋ within LiFePO4 underwent oxidation to form Fe3＋, with peak movement resembling that associated with the charging process of LiFePO4.

In the electric-field-induced assists leaching,
H
2
O2 not only oxidizes Fe2＋ to Fe3＋, which is conducive to the formation of FePO4 and promotes the dissolution of LiFePO4 and the release of Li but also releases O2 under high-temperature acidic conditions, which also accelerates the dissolution process of LiFePO4. If
H
2
O2 is not added, on the one hand, it will reduce the rate of Li, and also lead to the dissolution of Fe and Li together, reducing the selectivity of preferential lithium extraction. As illustrated in Fig. 8(e), Li and Fe leaching rates using the
H
2
SO4 +
H
2
O2 system without potential assistance are 73.22% and 0.92%, respectively. In contrast, when only current density is applied without the addition of acid, the leaching rates for Li and Fe decrease to 11.73% and 0.04%, respectively; this approach effectively reduces Fe leaching compared to conventional methods that do not utilize potential assistance. When both methods are combined, leaching in the
H
2
SO4 +
H
2
O2 system is enhanced by external potential. The results indicate a significant improvement in Li and Fe leaching rates, achieving values of 98.85% and 0.22%, respectively. Under controlled conditions with a specific
H
2
SO4 concentration, it is evident that increased potential assistance markedly enhances lithium extraction while simultaneously reducing iron leaching, thereby demonstrating high selectivity for lithium recovery.

Kinetic analyses were conducted to investigate the effects of varying temperatures and durations on the leaching rate of metals from waste lithium iron phosphate (LFP), to elucidate the mechanism underlying electric field-assisted lithium leaching. The leaching process involving solids and liquids can be described using a nuclear contraction model, which encompasses the following steps: (1) diffusion of the leaching agent through the boundary layer to reach the surface of LFP (external diffusion); (2) subsequent diffusion of the leaching agent through the solid film to access the surface of unreacted nuclei (internal diffusion); (3) reaction between the leaching agent and unreacted LFP; (4) diffusion of soluble Li
+
ions to reach the surface of LFP (internal diffusion); and (5) further diffusion of soluble Li
+
ions through the boundary layer into bulk solution (external diffusion) as outlined by (Jiang et al., 2021; Yang et al., 2022). Kinetic models for leaching are categorized into two types: surface chemical control model (Eq. (7)) and diffusion control model (Eq. (8)).
(7)
1 −
1 − x
1
3
=
k
1
t
(8)
1 − 2 / 3 x −
1 − x
2
3
=
k
2
t
Where x represents the leaching rate,t denotes the leaching time,
k
1
is the apparent rate constant associated with the dominant control of the chemical reaction, and
k
2
is the apparent rate constant related to diffusion control.

According to the Arrhenius equation, the rate constant remains k approximately constant at a given temperature. This relationship can be expressed in terms of absolute temperature as follows:
(9)
k = A
e
− E a / R T
(10)
l n K = l n A − E a / R T
Where k represents the apparent rate constant, A denotes the prefactor, (R is the gas constant (8.314 J/(mol K)), Ea signifies the apparent activation energy (kJ/mol), and T indicates the absolute temperature (K).

As illustrated in Fig. 9, the mean value of
R
1
2
is 0.983, while the mean value of
R
2
2
is 0.924. This indicates that the fit of the chemical reaction model surpasses that of the diffusion-controlled model, thereby confirming that surface chemical reactions govern the leaching process during electric field-assisted extraction. To gain a deeper understanding of the kinetic processes involved in these chemical reactions, we examined the apparent activation energy; a higher apparent activation energy suggests greater complexity in the reaction mechanism. We plotted lnk against 1000/T based on our fitted data. We estimated an apparent activation energy (Ea) of 35.06 kJ/mol using (Eq. (10)). This finding further supports our assertion that surface chemistry is critical in controlling lithium ion leaching processes.

At present, for the recycling of waste LFP, there are mainly fire recycling and wet recycling, for the waste LFP battery recycling mode, analyze the various costs of the recycling process, and establish the model of recycling profit in the form of a mathematical model with the following expression:
(11)
E = R − C
Where E is the total recycling profit; R is the total recycling revenue; and C is the treatment cost of recycled batteries. Where the processing cost of recycled batteries includes the following items:
(1) Raw material cost.
(2) Auxiliary material costs, such as acid, alkali, organic solvents, precipitants, etc. for treatment.
(3) Fuel power costs include electricity, natural gas, and water costs.
(4) Environmental treatment costs, such as the generation of waste gas, waste liquids, and residues, and the need for harmless treatment of the costs incurred.
(5) Labor costs.
(7) Equipment costs, such as maintenance and depreciation of equipment.
(8) Others. Site fees, common charges, taxes, etc.

In recent years, the share price trend of the battery recycling sector and lithium carbonate price fluctuations are closely associated, lithium carbonate for battery recycling is one of the main cost and revenue components, and its price fluctuations also directly affect the profit level of battery recycling enterprises. Recovery costs and product prices as in Table.S1 and Table.S2, the use of traditional thermal recycling technology to deal with each ton of waste batteries, LFP profit of $82.0, the use of wet recycling technology to deal with each ton of waste batteries, LFP loss of $-9.8, the use of potentiometric assisted method to deal with each ton of waste batteries, LFP profit of $65.2, Although the electric-field-induced assists method is slightly less profitable than the fire method, it has obvious advantages in environmental protection, energy consumption, recycling efficiency, and material purity, and can better respond to the national green recycling policy. The operation process is safer and more environmentally friendly, suitable for large-scale lithium iron phosphate battery recycling. Although the electric field-induced assisted leaching method requires a higher investment in technology and equipment, in the long run, its advantages make it a more sustainable and promising recovery method.

In this study, we have successfully achieved selective lithium recovery from waste lithium iron phosphate (LFP) cathode powder by integrating electric field-assisted and leaching techniques. Under optimal conditions, the lithium leaching rate reached 98.85%, while the iron leaching rate was only 0.22%, resulting in a selectivity of 99.8%. The
H
2
SO4 concentration and liquid–solid ratio were identified as the two most critical factors influencing these outcomes. Our experimental approach significantly enhanced efficiency while minimizing liquid waste generation compared to traditional leaching methods. Subsequently, lithium was recovered in the form of Li2CO3, and iron was retrieved as FePO4; both recovered products can serve as raw materials for new cathode materials. The feasibility of this experiment was assessed using the pH-potential diagram of the Li-Fe-P-
H
2
O system at 298.15 K. XPS and XRD analyses indicated that the lithium extraction process closely resembles the charging mechanism observed in LFP batteries, with FePO4 maintaining an olivine structure. Kinetic analysis revealed that surface chemical reactions predominantly controlled the assisted leaching process, leading to an estimated apparent activation energy of 35.06 kJ/mol. This study presents a novel recycling strategy for lithium iron phosphate batteries, contributing to advancements toward simple, efficient, and environmentally friendly battery recycling practices.
Shangpei Yong: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Conceptualization. Yan Lin: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52164049), the Yunnan Province Special Program for Building a South Asia-Southeast Asia Innovation Center (202303AP140019), Yunnan Sino-Laos International Joint R&D Center of Analysis and Technological Innovation for Energy, Mineral and Metallurgy .

ce:section.ce:para:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recycling of valuable metals from spent cathode material by organic pyrolysis combined with in-situ thermal reduction
